text.title: Remote Workshop Toolkit — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every remote / hybrid BA workshop must obey. These are the rules that compensate for the lack of a shared physical room — skipping any of them collapses the workshop into a meeting.

rule.statement: A workshop runs only after a 1-2 page pre-read has been circulated ≥48h before the live session AND ≥70% of attendees have acknowledged reading it (reaction, comment, or written pulse).

rationale.source: Amazon 6-pager practice; Lencioni — Death by Meeting

rule.rationale: In-person workshops absorb context cost in the room; remote workshops collapse if even 30% of attendees arrive unread. The pre-read shifts context to async, freeing live time for synthesis. The 70% acknowledgement floor is the early-warning that the workshop is mis-scheduled.

rule.statement: Every breakout MUST have: (a) named facilitator, (b) named timekeeper, (c) named scribe with explicit canvas frame, (d) a one-paragraph deliverable definition, (e) a read-out template the breakout completes before returning. No "go discuss" without the four roles.
rule.rationale: Unmoderated breakouts in remote workshops produce silence, off-topic chat, or domination by one voice. The four-role contract forces structure and the read-out template forces synthesis. Returning breakouts MUST present, not narrate.

rule.statement: Camera policy is stated in writing and acknowledged on the calendar invite. Default: cameras on for plenary, optional in breakouts. Mics on by default in breakouts of ≤6 people. "Mic muted, camera off, listening" is not a participation mode.

rationale.source: Stanford research on Zoom fatigue (Bailenson 2021); Microsoft hybrid-work studies

rule.rationale: The lurker is the default failure mode in remote workshops. A clear policy named in advance prevents the awkward live confrontation. Hybrid workshops require the in-room participants to use individual laptops to avoid the room-vs-remote asymmetry.

rule.statement: When the stakeholder cohort spans ≥3 time zones with no overlap window where ≥80% of attendees are in working hours, the workshop MUST be split into two sessions OR run with an explicit async-relay protocol (one session, the other gets recording + canvas to extend).

rationale.source: GitLab All-Remote Playbook; Doist async-first guidance

rule.rationale: A 6am or 11pm slot for any participant produces second-class participation. The 80% working-hours floor is the gate; splitting is cheaper than the silent quality loss.

rule.statement: For multi-session workshops, an async pulse (3-5 questions, ≤5 min completion) runs between sessions. ≥80% response rate is the threshold to proceed to the next live session; below that, schedule a follow-up rather than push forward.

rationale.source: Liberating Structures — TRIZ pattern; Atlassian Health Monitor

rule.rationale: Without pulses, multi-session workshops decay between sessions: people forget, decisions drift, blockers pile up. The pulse re-anchors and surfaces blockers before the next session has to deal with them live.

example.title: Hybrid kickoff workshop run-book excerpt
example.code: Pre-read sent: T-72h. Acknowledgement at T-24h: 9/12 (75%) — passes floor. Live session 1 (90 min, all in working hours by time-zone split rule): 00-10 Plenary, ground rules visible on canvas 10-30 Breakout A (4 people, eu-stakeholders) — process map as-is facilitator: Anna; timekeeper: Roman; scribe: Olha; canvas-frame: miro/process-map/as-is/eu; deliverable: 8-12 step process with hand-off owners 10-30 Breakout B (4 people, us-stakeholders) — same canvas, us scope 30-45 Read-out: each breakout presents using the template 45-75 Plenary synthesis (merge canvases) 75-90 Decisions list + parking lot + async-pulse plan Async pulse: T+24h, 4 questions, 11/12 responses (92%). Session 2: T+5d. Pre-read updated. Same protocol.
example.note: Illustrates r1-r5 in a single run.

example.title: The classic 90-minute meeting pretending to be a workshop
example.note: r1, r2, r3, r4 all violated. The workshop produced a recording, not a decision.
